title: while, do and for

publication.changeSummary: Language-specific C: Beginner to Intermediate draft for while, do and for; human standards, technical, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: GNU C Language Introduction and Reference Manual; accessed 2026-07; provisions applicable to while, do and for
sources.claim: C for, while, and do loops repeatedly evaluate defined control expressions, and correct loops maintain an invariant while making progress toward termination.

canonicalExplanation.summary: C for, while, and do loops repeatedly evaluate defined control expressions, and correct loops maintain an invariant while making progress toward termination.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A zero-based for loop visits indexes 0 through count - 1 using i < count and performs zero iterations when count is zero. Your goal is to write and debug counted, sentinel and input-driven loops. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Operators and precedence; pf control iteration; if and switch. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Language rule and operational model
sections.body: C for, while, and do loops repeatedly evaluate defined control expressions, and correct loops maintain an invariant while making progress toward termination. This rule is applied to concrete C source and observable behavior, not treated as a vocabulary definition alone.

sections.heading: Boundary and common failure
sections.body: Unsigned countdown, one-past array bounds, continue paths, mutation of control state, and empty input commonly break progress or coverage. Therefore the draft explicitly rejects this beginner failure model: The condition i <= count is the normal way to visit every element of an array with count elements.

includes: Choose and implement loops with explicit progress and termination.
Write and debug counted, sentinel and input-driven loops.
excludes: Unsigned countdown, one-past array bounds, continue paths, mutation of control state, and empty input commonly break progress or coverage.

outcomes: Write and debug counted, sentinel and input-driven loops.

examples.title: while, do and for: defined case
examples.situation: A zero-based for loop visits indexes 0 through count - 1 using i < count and performs zero iterations when count is zero.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Inspect the concrete source or state: A zero-based for loop visits indexes 0 through count - 1 using i < count and performs zero iterations when count is zero.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the conclusion: For zero-based count elements, iterate while i < count; count itself is one past the last valid index.
examples.result: The defined result follows because For zero-based count elements, iterate while i < count; count itself is one past the last valid index.

examples.title: while, do and for: boundary case
examples.situation: A do loop reads at least one menu choice before testing whether the user selected quit, matching the required first interaction.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Identify the changed source or state: A do loop reads at least one menu choice before testing whether the user selected quit, matching the required first interaction.
Step 2 — Keep this standard boundary visible: Unsigned countdown, one-past array bounds, continue paths, mutation of control state, and empty input commonly break progress or coverage.
Step 3 — Reject the tempting rule: The condition i <= count is the normal way to visit every element of an array with count elements.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Write and debug counted, sentinel and input-driven loops.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: The condition i <= count is the normal way to visit every element of an array with count elements.
misconceptions.correction: For zero-based count elements, iterate while i < count; count itself is one past the last valid index.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Using concrete C rules, explain why this belief fails: The condition i <= count is the normal way to visit every element of an array with count elements.

analogy.example: A manually managed workshop can model while, do and for: every tool, address, buffer, and acquired resource has an explicit valid range and owner.
usefulFor: Making low-level state, bounds, representation, ownership, and cleanup responsibilities concrete
limitations: C abstract-machine rules, undefined behavior, pointer provenance, translation, and implementation variation are stricter than a physical workshop analogy.
Unsigned countdown, one-past array bounds, continue paths, mutation of control state, and empty input commonly break progress or coverage.

recap.summary: You should now be able to write and debug counted, sentinel and input-driven loops. Keep this boundary in view: Unsigned countdown, one-past array bounds, continue paths, mutation of control state, and empty input commonly break progress or coverage.
retrievalPrompts: Explain while, do and for using a concrete C rule rather than the optional analogy.

Apply the checkpoint 'i < count' to the boundary case from memory.
recap.nextReviewPrompt: Next: continue to “break, continue and return”. Before moving on, answer one retrieval prompt without reopening the explanation.
